steps.title: Interpret conditional probability notation
steps.content: We want P(Site A | Red maple), which means: given that a tree is a red maple, what is the probability it comes from Site A? The condition restricts our focus to only red maple trees. We ignore chestnut oaks and site B entirely in the denominator.

steps.title: Extract the counts from the table
steps.content:
From the table, reading the Red maple row:
  • Red maples at Site A: 35
  • Red maples at Site B: 15
  • Total red maples: 50
These are the only values we need.

steps.title: Calculate the conditional probability
steps.content:
Out of all 50 red maples in the table, 35 come from Site A. Therefore:

$$P(\text{Site A} \mid \text{Red maple}) = \frac{35}{50} = \frac{7}{10} = 0.7$$

The answer is 0.7 or equivalently 7/10 or 35/50.
The answer is 0.7,7/10,35/50.
steps.formula: $$P(A \mid B) = \frac{\text{count of trees satisfying both A and B}}{\text{count of trees satisfying B}}$$
